saida_bruta:
“Your face seems familiar,” he remarked courteously. “Weren’t you with the First Division in the war?”

“Why, yes. I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I thought I’d seen you someplace before.”

For a while we spoke about several damp, dreary little French villages. Apparently he lived nearby, because he mentioned that he had recently purchased a hydroplane and intended to test it the next morning.

“Would you like to come with me, old sport? We’ll stay close to the shore along the Sound.”

“At what hour?”

“Whenever is most convenient for you.”

I was just about to ask his name when Jordan turned toward us and smiled.

“Enjoying yourself now?” she asked.

“Much more.” I faced my new companion again. “This is a strange sort of party for me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I gestured toward the unseen hedge far away, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation.”

For an instant he stared at me as though he did not understand.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not much of a host.”

saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he said politely. “Didn’t you serve with the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I did. I was in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I belonged to the Sixteenth until June, nineteen eighteen. I knew I had seen you somewhere.”

For some time we discussed a number of wet, miserable French villages. He evidently lived in the neighborhood, for he told me he had lately bought a hydroplane and planned to try it out the following morning.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? We’ll keep near the shore on the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Any time that suits you best.”

I was on the point of asking who he was when Jordan looked our way and smiled.

“Having a good time now?” she asked.

“Far better.” I turned again to my new acquaintance. “This is an odd kind of party for me. I haven’t even been introduced to the host. I live over there—” I pointed toward the distant, invisible hedge, “and this man Gatsby sent his chauffeur to invite me.”

For a moment he looked at me as if he could not comprehend.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I’m terribly sorry.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.”

I was about to ask him his name when Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Are you enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Much more.” I turned back toward my new companion. “This is a strange sort of party for me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I gestured toward the faraway, unseen hedge, “and Gatsby sent his chauffeur to invite me.”

For a moment he looked at me as though he hadn’t understood.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, I’m terribly sorry.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.

saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he said politely. “Weren’t you in the First Division during the war?”

“Yes. I served with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June nineteen eighteen. I knew I had seen you somewhere.”

For some time we spoke of several soggy, miserable villages in France. He apparently lived close by, since he said he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to try it out the following morning.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? We’ll keep near the shore along the Sound.”

“When?”

“Whenever it suits you.”

I was just about to ask his name when Jordan looked across and smiled.

“Are you having a better time now?” she asked.

“Far better.” I turned again to my new acquaintance. “This is an unusual kind of party for me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I motioned toward the distant, invisible hedge, “and Gatsby sent his chauffeur to ask me.”

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I’m awfully sorry.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not much of a host.”
